title: CXR — Approach
oneLiner: Systematic method to interpret chest X-rays; essential for diagnosing pulmonary, cardiac, and pleural pathology in acute care.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 68-year-old man with COPD presents to the emergency department with three days of progressive dyspnoea, cough productive of green sputum, and fever (38.5°C). He has reduced breath sounds and dullness to percussion over the right lower zone. Oxygen saturations are 90% on room air. A portable anterior-posterior (AP) chest X-ray is requested.

**Dissecting the vignette:**

- **Age + COPD** → higher risk of pneumonia, effusion, pneumothorax. Reduced baseline reserve means decompensation faster.
- **Progressive dyspnoea + productive cough + fever** → classic infective pattern. Green sputum suggests bacterial (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae).
- **Reduced breath sounds + dullness** → suggests consolidation or pleural effusion. With fever, likely consolidation (pneumonia) – effusion would more often have stony dullness and absent breath sounds rather than reduced.
- **Portable AP CXR** → limits quality: may exaggerate cardiac size, obscure subtle pneumothorax. Must adjust expectation.
- **Sat 90%** → significant hypoxaemia; CXR will guide need for antibiotics, NIV, or ICU.

**Pattern clues summary:**
| Clue | Tells you |
|------|-----------|
| Productive green sputum + fever | Bacterial pneumonia |
| Dullness + reduced breath sounds | Consolidation (or effusion) |
| COPD history | Emphysema, bullae, increased risk of pneumothorax |
| AP portable | Lower sensitivity for small pneumothorax, cardiomegaly over-called |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Silent hypoxia in young adult** – Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) in HIV: fever, dry cough, widespread interstitial infiltrates, but breath sounds may be clear; patient appears well until sudden desaturation.
- **Atypical chest pain only** – Aortic dissection: severe tearing interscapular pain, normal lung fields on CXR but widened mediastinum (>8 cm), often missed if focusing on lung.
- **Unexplained confusion in elderly** – Urinary tract infection causing sepsis but CXR shows subtle right lower lobe pneumonia (missed in up to 20% of seniors). Always check for aspiration in the frail.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
A chest X-ray is a two-dimensional projection of three-dimensional thoracic structures. The approach is a systematic reading method (often taught as "ABCC" or "RIPE") to ensure you do not miss critical findings. It is the single most common imaging study globally and the first line for dyspnoea, cough, chest pain, fever, trauma, and pre-operative assessment.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Every junior doctor will review dozens per week. Mistakes are common: missed pneumothorax, overlooked hilar mass, misinterpreted interstitial oedema.
- **Stakes:** A missed tension pneumothorax can be fatal within minutes; a missed early cancer delays treatment by months.
- **Pitfalls:** Over-reliance on the report (which may be delayed), reading without clinical context, and failing to compare to old films are the top three errors.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
An X-ray image is created by differential attenuation of X-rays through tissues. Air attenuates least (black), bone most (white), with soft tissue and fluid in between (grey). Every radiographic pattern arises from this principle:

- **Air bronchogram:** When alveoli are filled with fluid/infection (consolidation), they become radio-opaque (white), but the air-filled bronchi remain black. Result: branching black lines within white lung – pathognomonic for airspace disease (pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, ALI).
- **Silhouette sign:** Loss of the normal soft-tissue border (e.g., right heart border, diaphragm) occurs when adjacent air-filled lung is replaced by a radiodensity of similar density. Example: right middle lobe pneumonia blurs the right heart border because it lies directly anterior to the heart.
- **Pleural effusion:** Fluid in the pleural space layers posteriorly and laterally on an erect PA film, causing blunting of the costophrenic angle (first sign on PA) or a meniscus sign on lateral. Supine – fluid layers posteriorly, making the lung appear hazy (white-out) without blunting.
- **Pneumothorax:** Air in pleural space separates visceral from parietal pleura. On erect film, air rises to the apex – deep sulcus sign (hyperlucent costophrenic angle) on supine. The visceral pleural line is the key visible edge.
- **Cardiomegaly:** Cardiothoracic ratio >0.5 on PA film. But on AP portable, the heart appears larger due to magnification (anterior placement). Know this trap.

**Connecting pathophysiology to pattern recognition:**
- Fever + productive cough + air bronchogram → consolidation → pneumonia.
- Dyspnoea + raised JVP + Kerley B lines (horizontal short lines at lung bases) → interstitial oedema → pulmonary oedema.
- Trauma + hyperlucent hemithorax + deep sulcus → pneumothorax.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
Because "CXR approach" is a skill, this section will list common findings you encounter and their etiologies with stem clues.

| Category | Common finding | Stem clue |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Infectious** | Consolidation (lobar/segmental) | Fever, cough, sputum, leucocytosis |
| **Infectious** | Interstitial infiltrates (atypical) | Dry cough, insidious onset, HIV/immunosuppression |
| **Cardiogenic** | Pulmonary oedema (bat-wing perihilar) | Orthopnoea, PND, raised JVP, pedal oedema |
| **Neoplastic** | Hilar mass / solitary pulmonary nodule | Smoker, weight loss, haemoptysis |
| **Pleural** | Effusion (unilateral/bilateral) | Dyspnoea, dullness, +/- fever (exudate if infection) |
| **Pleural** | Pneumothorax | Sudden pleuritic pain, dyspnoea, absent breath sounds |
| **Vascular** | Widened mediastinum (aortic dissection) | Tearing chest pain, pulse deficit, hypotension |
| **Trauma** | Rib fractures / haemothorax | History of trauma, pain, hyperlucent zones |
| **Autoimmune** | Bilateral interstitial fibrosis (IPF) | Chronic dyspnoea, dry cough, clubbing |
| **Iatrogenic** | Central line malposition / pneumothorax | Post-procedure, sudden respiratory distress |

**Most common cause of an abnormal CXR in acute medicine:** Pneumonia (community-acquired).
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
In the context of a CXR, the "investigation" is the X-ray itself, but you must also integrate it with other data. There is no specific diagnostic criteria for "chest X-ray approach", but there are systematic reading protocols.

**Systematic approach (ABCDE – adapted for CXR):**

| Step | What | Why | How to interpret |
|------|------|-----|------------------|
| **A – Airway** | Check for tracheal deviation | Shift can indicate tension pneumothorax (away), large effusion (same side), collapse (toward lesion) | Palpate in person; on film, trace trachea from vocal cords to carina. |
| **B – Breathing / Lungs** | Inspect lung fields: symmetry, haziness, lines | Detect consolidation, effusion, pneumothorax, fibrosis | Compare left vs right. Use silhouette sign to localise. |
| **C – Cardiac** | Assess heart size, borders, silhouette | Cardiomegaly, pericardial effusion, chamber enlargement | Cardiothoracic ratio >0.5 on PA (false on AP). |
| **D – Diaphragm** | Check costophrenic angles, domes | Effusion, pneumoperitoneum, diaphragmatic paralysis | Angle sharp? Blunted? Free air under diaphragm? |
| **E – Everything else** | Chest wall, bones, soft tissue, tubes/lines | Rib fractures, surgical emphysema, line position | Trace every tube to tip (ETT 2-3 cm above carina; CVC tip in SVC) |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms:**
- **Air bronchogram** = alveolar filling (pneumonia, oedema, haemorrhage – require clinical context).
- **Pleural line** on erect film = pneumothorax (unless skin fold – careful).
- **Kerley B lines** = interstitial oedema (congestive heart failure).

**What rules out:**
- A normal CXR with typical symptoms does **not** rule out pulmonary embolism (PE) – >50% of PEs have normal X-ray.
- A normal CXR does not rule out early pneumonia (especially in dehydrated patients).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
Not disease management, but the approach to reading the CXR and acting on findings.

**Immediate (first hour) – what + why:**
- **Check patient details, date, view (PA vs AP, erect vs supine).** Why: AP exaggerates heart size; supine misses small pneumothorax.
- **Assess technical adequacy:** Is the film penetrated? Check vertebrae are just visible behind heart. Underpenetrated = white lungs mimic disease; overpenetrated = black lungs miss effusions.
- **Look for life-threatening findings first** – tension pneumothorax (tracheal deviation, hyperlucent hemithorax, hypotension), massive haemothorax (opaque hemithorax with mediastinal shift), pericardial tamponade (globular heart, clear lungs) – before systematic read.

**Ward (24–72 h) – what + why:**
- **Systematic read using ABCDE** as above. Document in notes: "CXR reviewed: no pneumothorax, no effusion, heart size normal, clear lung fields." This forces thoroughness.
- **Compare with previous films** – a new opacity is far more significant than a chronic one. Old films are the single best tool.
- **Integrate clinical context** – a small pneumothorax in a young healthy person may be observed; in a ventilated patient it requires urgent drainage.

**Long-term:**
- **For incidental findings** (solitary pulmonary nodule >8 mm) – refer CT chest and pulmonary nodule clinic.
- **For chronic interstitial changes** – liaise with respiratory medicine, arrange high-resolution CT (HRCT).

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- Don't rely solely on the radiologist's report – false negative rates ~10-15% for junior reports; always view yourself.
- Don't miss a small pneumothorax on a supine film – check for deep sulcus sign.
- Don't call "cardiomegaly" on an AP film – check if PA.
- Don't assume bilateral lower lobe consolidation is pneumonia – consider pulmonary oedema.

**Drug doses in context** (if finding indicates treatment):
- CAP: Amoxicillin 500 mg TDS po (or IV) 5 days; add Clarithromycin 500 mg BD if atypical cover needed.
- Pulmonary oedema: Furosemide 40 mg IV stat, then 40 mg BD po. Nitrates if hypertensive.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
This table compares common CXR findings and their mimics (consolidation vs atelectasis vs effusion vs mass). The "feature" column refers to radiographic characteristics.

| Feature | Consolidation (Pneumonia) | Atelectasis | Pleural Effusion | Solitary Pulmonary Nodule |
|---------|---------------------------|-------------|------------------|---------------------------|
| **Opacity** | Air bronchogram positive – branching air within white | Increased opacity, often triangular, without air bronchogram | Homogeneous opacity, meniscus sign on erect, blunted CP angle | Well-circumscribed round opacity, no air bronchogram |
| **Volume effect** | No volume loss; sometimes lung expanded | Volume loss: fissure displacement, mediastinal shift toward lesion | Volume loss minimal; mediastinal shift away if massive | No volume loss unless causing collapse |
| **Border** | Obscures adjacent structures (silhouette) | Sharply defined by fissure | Sharp upper border (meniscus) | Discrete, smooth or spiculated |
| **Clinical** | Fever, cough, sputum | Post-op, mucus plug, sudden dyspnoea | Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, dullness | Asymptomatic (incidental) or haemoptysis |
| **Key differentiator** | Air bronchogram + fever | Volume loss + no air bronchogram | Meniscus + blunted CP angle + fluid on lateral | CT further characterisation; PET if malignant suspicion |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Missed tension pneumothorax:**
- **Presentation:** Agitated, tracheal deviation away, distended neck veins, hypotension, contralateral hyperresonance.
- **Key signs:** On CXR – deep sulcus sign (on supine), visceral pleural line, mediastinal shift. If untreated: cardiac arrest within minutes.
- **What to do:** Immediate needle decompression (2nd intercostal space midclavicular line, large bore cannula) then chest drain.

**Missed lower lobe pneumonia (especially in elderly):**
- **Presentation:** Confusion, falls, reduced oral intake – no fever or cough in many.
- **Key signs:** Loss of left/right hemidiaphragm silhouette (lower lobe consolidation). Often missed on AP portable.
- **Outcome:** Progresses to sepsis, respiratory failure, death.

**Missed pulmonary oedema (early interstitial):**
- **Presentation:** Gradual dyspnoea, orthopnoea, raised JVP – but early stage may have clear lungs on auscultation.
- **Key signs:** Kerley B lines, perihilar haziness, upper lobe diversion. If missed: overt cardiogenic shock.

**Missed solitary pulmonary nodule (cancer):**
- **Presentation:** Incidental on CXR for other reason – often ignored if not reported.
- **Key signs:** >8 mm nodule, spiculated margins, no prior film. If missed: delayed diagnosis reduces 5-year survival from 50% to <10%.
- **What to do:** CT chest, PET, bronchoscopy, or CT-guided biopsy.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **RIPE** – Quality check: **R**ight (patient label), **I**nspiration (count anterior ribs: 5-7 visible anteriorly), **P**enetration (vertebrae visible behind heart), **E**xposure (rotation: clavicles symmetric).
- **ABCDE** – Systematic read: **A**irway, **B**reathing, **C**ardiac, **D**iaphragm, **E**verything else.
- **SILLY** – Signs of pneumothorax: **S**udden onset pain, **I**nspiratory intercostal bulge, **L**oss of breath sounds, **L**oud hyperresonance, **Y**awning (tracheal deviation).

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Silhouette sign is your friend** – loss of right heart border = right middle lobe disease; loss of left heart border = lingular disease; loss of diaphragm = lower lobe disease.
2. **Air bronchogram = always pathological** (pneumonia, oedema, haemorrhage, ARDS) – it never occurs in normal lung.
3. **A normal CXR does not rule out PE, small pneumothorax, or early pneumonia** (especially if dehydrated).
4. **Supine CXR: pneumothorax presents as deep sulcus sign** – look for hyperlucent deep costophrenic angle, not apical blackness.
5. **Comparison with old films is the single most valuable step** – a new opacity is far more concerning than a chronic scar.
6. **Bilateral basal infiltrates in the elderly** – consider pulmonary oedema before pneumonia (look for Kerley B lines, central bat-wing distribution).
7. **ETT should be 2-3 cm above carina** – too high: vocal cord injury; too low: mainstem intubation (usually right).

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Calling a skin fold a pneumothorax – skin fold has no visceral pleural line, extends beyond lung, and has no underlying lung edge.
- Reporting "cardiomegaly" on an AP portable film without caveat – always note "AP view; heart size cannot be accurately assessed."
- Missing the right paratracheal stripe >5 mm – suggests mediastinal mass or nodes (lymphoma, sarcoid).
- Not checking rotation – rotated film can mimic mediastinal shift or cardiomegaly.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior (registrar or consultant):**
- Suspected **tension pneumothorax** (immediate needle decompression needed; call senior after starting)
- **Massive haemothorax** (opaque hemithorax with mediastinal shift – needs drain and urgent referral)
- **Widened mediastinum >8 cm** (possible dissection – call cardiothoracic)
- **Pericardial tamponade** (globular heart, clear lungs, hypotension – call cardiology/echo)
- **Incidental large thoracic aortic aneurysm** (usually asymptomatic but rupture risk – referral)

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- **NEWS2 ≥7** (any single red parameter: RR >24, SpO2 <91% on air, systolic BP <90, HR >130, new confusion, or lactate >4)
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (sepsis, hypoperfusion)
- **GCS ≤8** (intubate and protect airway)
- **Respiratory acidosis** (pH <7.25, PaCO2 >60 mmHg despite NIV – need mechanical ventilation)
- **Refractory hypotension** despite 30 mL/kg crystalloid and vasopressors

**Specific thresholds for CXR findings:**
- Pneumothorax >2 cm from lung apex on inspiratory film (or any tension) – chest drain.
- Pleural effusion causing dyspnoea or >1/3 of hemithorax – consider drainage.
- Any new pulmonary opacity in neutropenic or immunocompromised patient – urgent microbiology, CT, broad-spectrum antifungals.

rawMarkdown:
## CXR — Approach

**One-liner:** Systematic method to interpret chest X-rays; essential for diagnosing pulmonary, cardiac, and pleural pathology in acute care.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 68-year-old man with COPD presents to the emergency department with three days of progressive dyspnoea, cough productive of green sputum, and fever (38.5°C). He has reduced breath sounds and dullness to percussion over the right lower zone. Oxygen saturations are 90% on room air. A portable anterior-posterior (AP) chest X-ray is requested.

**Dissecting the vignette:**

- **Age + COPD** → higher risk of pneumonia, effusion, pneumothorax. Reduced baseline reserve means decompensation faster.
- **Progressive dyspnoea + productive cough + fever** → classic infective pattern. Green sputum suggests bacterial (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae).
- **Reduced breath sounds + dullness** → suggests consolidation or pleural effusion. With fever, likely consolidation (pneumonia) – effusion would more often have stony dullness and absent breath sounds rather than reduced.
- **Portable AP CXR** → limits quality: may exaggerate cardiac size, obscure subtle pneumothorax. Must adjust expectation.
- **Sat 90%** → significant hypoxaemia; CXR will guide need for antibiotics, NIV, or ICU.

**Pattern clues summary:**
| Clue | Tells you |
|------|-----------|
| Productive green sputum + fever | Bacterial pneumonia |
| Dullness + reduced breath sounds | Consolidation (or effusion) |
| COPD history | Emphysema, bullae, increased risk of pneumothorax |
| AP portable | Lower sensitivity for small pneumothorax, cardiomegaly over-called |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Silent hypoxia in young adult** – Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) in HIV: fever, dry cough, widespread interstitial infiltrates, but breath sounds may be clear; patient appears well until sudden desaturation.
- **Atypical chest pain only** – Aortic dissection: severe tearing interscapular pain, normal lung fields on CXR but widened mediastinum (>8 cm), often missed if focusing on lung.
- **Unexplained confusion in elderly** – Urinary tract infection causing sepsis but CXR shows subtle right lower lobe pneumonia (missed in up to 20% of seniors). Always check for aspiration in the frail.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

A chest X-ray is a two-dimensional projection of three-dimensional thoracic structures. The approach is a systematic reading method (often taught as "ABCC" or "RIPE") to ensure you do not miss critical findings. It is the single most common imaging study globally and the first line for dyspnoea, cough, chest pain, fever, trauma, and pre-operative assessment.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Every junior doctor will review dozens per week. Mistakes are common: missed pneumothorax, overlooked hilar mass, misinterpreted interstitial oedema.
- **Stakes:** A missed tension pneumothorax can be fatal within minutes; a missed early cancer delays treatment by months.
- **Pitfalls:** Over-reliance on the report (which may be delayed), reading without clinical context, and failing to compare to old films are the top three errors.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

An X-ray image is created by differential attenuation of X-rays through tissues. Air attenuates least (black), bone most (white), with soft tissue and fluid in between (grey). Every radiographic pattern arises from this principle:

- **Air bronchogram:** When alveoli are filled with fluid/infection (consolidation), they become radio-opaque (white), but the air-filled bronchi remain black. Result: branching black lines within white lung – pathognomonic for airspace disease (pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, ALI).
- **Silhouette sign:** Loss of the normal soft-tissue border (e.g., right heart border, diaphragm) occurs when adjacent air-filled lung is replaced by a radiodensity of similar density. Example: right middle lobe pneumonia blurs the right heart border because it lies directly anterior to the heart.
- **Pleural effusion:** Fluid in the pleural space layers posteriorly and laterally on an erect PA film, causing blunting of the costophrenic angle (first sign on PA) or a meniscus sign on lateral. Supine – fluid layers posteriorly, making the lung appear hazy (white-out) without blunting.
- **Pneumothorax:** Air in pleural space separates visceral from parietal pleura. On erect film, air rises to the apex – deep sulcus sign (hyperlucent costophrenic angle) on supine. The visceral pleural line is the key visible edge.
- **Cardiomegaly:** Cardiothoracic ratio >0.5 on PA film. But on AP portable, the heart appears larger due to magnification (anterior placement). Know this trap.

**Connecting pathophysiology to pattern recognition:**
- Fever + productive cough + air bronchogram → consolidation → pneumonia.
- Dyspnoea + raised JVP + Kerley B lines (horizontal short lines at lung bases) → interstitial oedema → pulmonary oedema.
- Trauma + hyperlucent hemithorax + deep sulcus → pneumothorax.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

Because "CXR approach" is a skill, this section will list common findings you encounter and their etiologies with stem clues.

| Category | Common finding | Stem clue |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Infectious** | Consolidation (lobar/segmental) | Fever, cough, sputum, leucocytosis |
| **Infectious** | Interstitial infiltrates (atypical) | Dry cough, insidious onset, HIV/immunosuppression |
| **Cardiogenic** | Pulmonary oedema (bat-wing perihilar) | Orthopnoea, PND, raised JVP, pedal oedema |
| **Neoplastic** | Hilar mass / solitary pulmonary nodule | Smoker, weight loss, haemoptysis |
| **Pleural** | Effusion (unilateral/bilateral) | Dyspnoea, dullness, +/- fever (exudate if infection) |
| **Pleural** | Pneumothorax | Sudden pleuritic pain, dyspnoea, absent breath sounds |
| **Vascular** | Widened mediastinum (aortic dissection) | Tearing chest pain, pulse deficit, hypotension |
| **Trauma** | Rib fractures / haemothorax | History of trauma, pain, hyperlucent zones |
| **Autoimmune** | Bilateral interstitial fibrosis (IPF) | Chronic dyspnoea, dry cough, clubbing |
| **Iatrogenic** | Central line malposition / pneumothorax | Post-procedure, sudden respiratory distress |

**Most common cause of an abnormal CXR in acute medicine:** Pneumonia (community-acquired).

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

In the context of a CXR, the "investigation" is the X-ray itself, but you must also integrate it with other data. There is no specific diagnostic criteria for "chest X-ray approach", but there are systematic reading protocols.

**Systematic approach (ABCDE – adapted for CXR):**

| Step | What | Why | How to interpret |
|------|------|-----|------------------|
| **A – Airway** | Check for tracheal deviation | Shift can indicate tension pneumothorax (away), large effusion (same side), collapse (toward lesion) | Palpate in person; on film, trace trachea from vocal cords to carina. |
| **B – Breathing / Lungs** | Inspect lung fields: symmetry, haziness, lines | Detect consolidation, effusion, pneumothorax, fibrosis | Compare left vs right. Use silhouette sign to localise. |
| **C – Cardiac** | Assess heart size, borders, silhouette | Cardiomegaly, pericardial effusion, chamber enlargement | Cardiothoracic ratio >0.5 on PA (false on AP). |
| **D – Diaphragm** | Check costophrenic angles, domes | Effusion, pneumoperitoneum, diaphragmatic paralysis | Angle sharp? Blunted? Free air under diaphragm? |
| **E – Everything else** | Chest wall, bones, soft tissue, tubes/lines | Rib fractures, surgical emphysema, line position | Trace every tube to tip (ETT 2-3 cm above carina; CVC tip in SVC) |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms:**
- **Air bronchogram** = alveolar filling (pneumonia, oedema, haemorrhage – require clinical context).
- **Pleural line** on erect film = pneumothorax (unless skin fold – careful).
- **Kerley B lines** = interstitial oedema (congestive heart failure).

**What rules out:**
- A normal CXR with typical symptoms does **not** rule out pulmonary embolism (PE) – >50% of PEs have normal X-ray.
- A normal CXR does not rule out early pneumonia (especially in dehydrated patients).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

Not disease management, but the approach to reading the CXR and acting on findings.

**Immediate (first hour) – what + why:**
- **Check patient details, date, view (PA vs AP, erect vs supine).** Why: AP exaggerates heart size; supine misses small pneumothorax.
- **Assess technical adequacy:** Is the film penetrated? Check vertebrae are just visible behind heart. Underpenetrated = white lungs mimic disease; overpenetrated = black lungs miss effusions.
- **Look for life-threatening findings first** – tension pneumothorax (tracheal deviation, hyperlucent hemithorax, hypotension), massive haemothorax (opaque hemithorax with mediastinal shift), pericardial tamponade (globular heart, clear lungs) – before systematic read.

**Ward (24–72 h) – what + why:**
- **Systematic read using ABCDE** as above. Document in notes: "CXR reviewed: no pneumothorax, no effusion, heart size normal, clear lung fields." This forces thoroughness.
- **Compare with previous films** – a new opacity is far more significant than a chronic one. Old films are the single best tool.
- **Integrate clinical context** – a small pneumothorax in a young healthy person may be observed; in a ventilated patient it requires urgent drainage.

**Long-term:**
- **For incidental findings** (solitary pulmonary nodule >8 mm) – refer CT chest and pulmonary nodule clinic.
- **For chronic interstitial changes** – liaise with respiratory medicine, arrange high-resolution CT (HRCT).

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- Don't rely solely on the radiologist's report – false negative rates ~10-15% for junior reports; always view yourself.
- Don't miss a small pneumothorax on a supine film – check for deep sulcus sign.
- Don't call "cardiomegaly" on an AP film – check if PA.
- Don't assume bilateral lower lobe consolidation is pneumonia – consider pulmonary oedema.

**Drug doses in context** (if finding indicates treatment):
- CAP: Amoxicillin 500 mg TDS po (or IV) 5 days; add Clarithromycin 500 mg BD if atypical cover needed.
- Pulmonary oedema: Furosemide 40 mg IV stat, then 40 mg BD po. Nitrates if hypertensive.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

This table compares common CXR findings and their mimics (consolidation vs atelectasis vs effusion vs mass). The "feature" column refers to radiographic characteristics.

| Feature | Consolidation (Pneumonia) | Atelectasis | Pleural Effusion | Solitary Pulmonary Nodule |
|---------|---------------------------|-------------|------------------|---------------------------|
| **Opacity** | Air bronchogram positive – branching air within white | Increased opacity, often triangular, without air bronchogram | Homogeneous opacity, meniscus sign on erect, blunted CP angle | Well-circumscribed round opacity, no air bronchogram |
| **Volume effect** | No volume loss; sometimes lung expanded | Volume loss: fissure displacement, mediastinal shift toward lesion | Volume loss minimal; mediastinal shift away if massive | No volume loss unless causing collapse |
| **Border** | Obscures adjacent structures (silhouette) | Sharply defined by fissure | Sharp upper border (meniscus) | Discrete, smooth or spiculated |
| **Clinical** | Fever, cough, sputum | Post-op, mucus plug, sudden dyspnoea | Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, dullness | Asymptomatic (incidental) or haemoptysis |
| **Key differentiator** | Air bronchogram + fever | Volume loss + no air bronchogram | Meniscus + blunted CP angle + fluid on lateral | CT further characterisation; PET if malignant suspicion |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Missed tension pneumothorax:**
- **Presentation:** Agitated, tracheal deviation away, distended neck veins, hypotension, contralateral hyperresonance.
- **Key signs:** On CXR – deep sulcus sign (on supine), visceral pleural line, mediastinal shift. If untreated: cardiac arrest within minutes.
- **What to do:** Immediate needle decompression (2nd intercostal space midclavicular line, large bore cannula) then chest drain.

**Missed lower lobe pneumonia (especially in elderly):**
- **Presentation:** Confusion, falls, reduced oral intake – no fever or cough in many.
- **Key signs:** Loss of left/right hemidiaphragm silhouette (lower lobe consolidation). Often missed on AP portable.
- **Outcome:** Progresses to sepsis, respiratory failure, death.

**Missed pulmonary oedema (early interstitial):**
- **Presentation:** Gradual dyspnoea, orthopnoea, raised JVP – but early stage may have clear lungs on auscultation.
- **Key signs:** Kerley B lines, perihilar haziness, upper lobe diversion. If missed: overt cardiogenic shock.

**Missed solitary pulmonary nodule (cancer):**
- **Presentation:** Incidental on CXR for other reason – often ignored if not reported.
- **Key signs:** >8 mm nodule, spiculated margins, no prior film. If missed: delayed diagnosis reduces 5-year survival from 50% to <10%.
- **What to do:** CT chest, PET, bronchoscopy, or CT-guided biopsy.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **RIPE** – Quality check: **R**ight (patient label), **I**nspiration (count anterior ribs: 5-7 visible anteriorly), **P**enetration (vertebrae visible behind heart), **E**xposure (rotation: clavicles symmetric).
- **ABCDE** – Systematic read: **A**irway, **B**reathing, **C**ardiac, **D**iaphragm, **E**verything else.
- **SILLY** – Signs of pneumothorax: **S**udden onset pain, **I**nspiratory intercostal bulge, **L**oss of breath sounds, **L**oud hyperresonance, **Y**awning (tracheal deviation).

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Silhouette sign is your friend** – loss of right heart border = right middle lobe disease; loss of left heart border = lingular disease; loss of diaphragm = lower lobe disease.
2. **Air bronchogram = always pathological** (pneumonia, oedema, haemorrhage, ARDS) – it never occurs in normal lung.
3. **A normal CXR does not rule out PE, small pneumothorax, or early pneumonia** (especially if dehydrated).
4. **Supine CXR: pneumothorax presents as deep sulcus sign** – look for hyperlucent deep costophrenic angle, not apical blackness.
5. **Comparison with old films is the single most valuable step** – a new opacity is far more concerning than a chronic scar.
6. **Bilateral basal infiltrates in the elderly** – consider pulmonary oedema before pneumonia (look for Kerley B lines, central bat-wing distribution).
7. **ETT should be 2-3 cm above carina** – too high: vocal cord injury; too low: mainstem intubation (usually right).

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Calling a skin fold a pneumothorax – skin fold has no visceral pleural line, extends beyond lung, and has no underlying lung edge.
- Reporting "cardiomegaly" on an AP portable film without caveat – always note "AP view; heart size cannot be accurately assessed."
- Missing the right paratracheal stripe >5 mm – suggests mediastinal mass or nodes (lymphoma, sarcoid).
- Not checking rotation – rotated film can mimic mediastinal shift or cardiomegaly.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior (registrar or consultant):**
- Suspected **tension pneumothorax** (immediate needle decompression needed; call senior after starting)
- **Massive haemothorax** (opaque hemithorax with mediastinal shift – needs drain and urgent referral)
- **Widened mediastinum >8 cm** (possible dissection – call cardiothoracic)
- **Pericardial tamponade** (globular heart, clear lungs, hypotension – call cardiology/echo)
- **Incidental large thoracic aortic aneurysm** (usually asymptomatic but rupture risk – referral)

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- **NEWS2 ≥7** (any single red parameter: RR >24, SpO2 <91% on air, systolic BP <90, HR >130, new confusion, or lactate >4)
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (sepsis, hypoperfusion)
- **GCS ≤8** (intubate and protect airway)
- **Respiratory acidosis** (pH <7.25, PaCO2 >60 mmHg despite NIV – need mechanical ventilation)
- **Refractory hypotension** despite 30 mL/kg crystalloid and vasopressors

**Specific thresholds for CXR findings:**
- Pneumothorax >2 cm from lung apex on inspiratory film (or any tension) – chest drain.
- Pleural effusion causing dyspnoea or >1/3 of hemithorax – consider drainage.
- Any new pulmonary opacity in neutropenic or immunocompromised patient – urgent microbiology, CT, broad-spectrum antifungals.
